stem: Your synthesis agent receives aggregated findings from six research subagents as one long input: roughly 80,000 tokens of raw findings concatenated in the order the subagents finished. QA review shows the final reports reliably incorporate findings that happen to sit near the beginning or end of the aggregated input, but findings from the middle sections are omitted from the report about a third of the time, even though they are present in the input. You need to change how the aggregated input is assembled. Which approach most directly mitigates this failure mode?
correct_choice: Place a key-findings summary at the beginning of the aggregated input, then organize the detailed results under explicit section headers so critical information is anchored where the model processes it reliably.
correct_rationale: This is the documented mitigation for the 'lost in the middle' effect: models reliably process information at the beginning and end of long inputs but may omit findings from middle sections. Leading with a summary puts every critical finding in the high-attention region, and explicit section headers give the model structural anchors for locating detail in the middle.
near_miss_choice: Sort the subagent findings by relevance score so the most important findings appear first, and truncate the input to the top 50% to keep it comfortably under the context limit.
near_miss_rationale: Ordering by relevance helps the top findings but truncating discards half the research outright — the problem was omission of valid middle-section findings, and this approach guarantees their loss instead of fixing their placement. The input already fits in context; length is not the failure mode, position is.
distractor1_choice: Split the synthesis into six separate API calls, one per subagent's findings, and concatenate the six partial reports into the final document.
distractor1_rationale: Per-subagent synthesis removes the long input but eliminates cross-source synthesis entirely — the synthesis agent can no longer compare, reconcile, or merge findings across subagents, which is the reason a synthesis stage exists. Concatenated partial reports are not a synthesized report.
distractor2_choice: Increase max_tokens on the synthesis request so the model has enough output budget to cover every finding in the input.
distractor2_rationale: max_tokens caps the response length; it has no effect on which parts of the input the model attends to. The reports were not running out of output budget — they were omitting middle-section findings, which is an input-position effect, not an output-length limit.
anti_pattern: Feeding a long undifferentiated concatenation of findings to a synthesis step and assuming every section gets equal attention, instead of placing key findings at the beginning and structuring the middle with explicit headers to counter the lost-in-the-middle effect.

stem: Your support agent handles long multi-issue conversations. To control context growth, older turns are progressively summarized. After the change, agents working late in a conversation start making errors on specifics: quoting a refund amount of "around $180" when the customer was told $187.50, and referencing "your recent order" when two order numbers (#88231 and #88764) are in play. The summaries describe the conversation accurately in broad strokes but the transactional specifics are getting blurred. What is the most effective fix?
correct_choice: Extract transactional facts — amounts, dates, order numbers, statuses — into a persistent case-facts block that is included verbatim in every prompt, outside the summarized history.
correct_rationale: Progressive summarization condenses numerical values, dates, and customer-stated specifics into vague prose — that is exactly the observed failure. A structured case-facts block carried verbatim in each prompt keeps the exact figures available regardless of how aggressively the narrative history is summarized.
near_miss_choice: Instruct the summarizer prompt to always preserve all numbers, dates, and identifiers exactly when condensing older turns.
near_miss_rationale: This relies on probabilistic instruction-following inside repeated lossy compression: every summarization pass is another chance for a figure to be dropped or rounded, and once lost it cannot be recovered from the summary. Separating facts from the summarized narrative removes them from the lossy path entirely instead of asking the compressor to be careful.
distractor1_choice: Stop summarizing and pass the full conversation history on every request so no detail is ever condensed.
distractor1_rationale: Passing the full transcript restores the specifics but abandons context management: long multi-issue conversations grow without bound, costs rise with every turn, and very long histories reintroduce position effects. Summarization was added for a reason; the fix is protecting critical facts from it, not abandoning it.
distractor2_choice: Lower the temperature on the support agent's requests so it reproduces figures from the summarized history more deterministically.
distractor2_rationale: Temperature affects sampling variability, not information availability. The exact amount $187.50 is no longer present in the summarized context — no decoding setting can recover information that summarization already discarded.
anti_pattern: Letting progressive summarization be the only carrier of transactional specifics, so amounts, dates, and order numbers get condensed into vague summaries instead of being extracted into a persistent structured facts block outside the summarized history.

stem: Reviewing transcripts of your support resolution agent, you find this pattern: a customer opens with "I've been going back and forth on this for a week. Please just connect me to a human." The agent responds by asking for the order number, runs get_customer and lookup_order, proposes a resolution, and only offers escalation after the customer repeats the demand a second time. Your escalation policy review flags this conversation. What should the agent have done?
correct_choice: Escalate to a human immediately on the explicit request, without first attempting investigation or resolution.
correct_rationale: An explicit customer demand for a human is itself an escalation trigger. Honoring it immediately respects the customer's stated preference; forcing them through an investigation they already declined compounds frustration and delays the handoff they asked for.
near_miss_choice: Acknowledge the frustration, briefly offer to resolve the issue since it may be within the agent's capability, and escalate if the customer reiterates the request.
near_miss_rationale: Acknowledge-then-offer is the right pattern when the customer expresses frustration but has not asked for a human — there, offering resolution for a straightforward issue is appropriate, escalating only if they reiterate. Here the customer explicitly demanded a human up front, which calls for immediate escalation, not a counter-offer.
distractor1_choice: Continue handling the case autonomously but adopt an empathetic tone, since the issue history suggests it is resolvable without human involvement.
distractor1_rationale: Whether the issue is technically resolvable is not the deciding factor once the customer has explicitly asked for a human. Overriding a stated preference because the agent judges itself capable is exactly the behavior that erodes trust in automated support.
distractor2_choice: Run sentiment analysis on the message and escalate only if the frustration score exceeds the configured threshold.
distractor2_rationale: No sentiment scoring is needed to interpret "connect me to a human" — it is an explicit request, not a signal to be inferred. Sentiment thresholds are also unreliable proxies for escalation in general; gating an explicit demand behind one adds a failure mode where the request is ignored.
anti_pattern: Attempting investigation and resolution before honoring an explicit customer request for a human agent, treating escalation as a last resort instead of respecting the stated preference immediately.

stem: Your support agent's get_customer tool accepts a name and email. A customer writes in as "Chris Martin" from a shared family email address, and the lookup returns two customer records: one with an open order for a jacket, one with an order delivered last month. The refund request says only "my order arrived damaged." Logs show the agent selected the record with the open order because "an open order is more likely the subject of a support request," and began the damage-claim workflow against it. What behavior should the agent have exhibited instead?
correct_choice: Ask the customer for an additional identifier — such as the order number or billing ZIP — to disambiguate between the two matching records before proceeding.
correct_rationale: When a lookup returns multiple matches, the agent must request additional identifiers rather than select a record by heuristic. Guessing wrong here means processing a refund against the wrong customer's order — a financial action taken on a misidentified account.
near_miss_choice: Proceed with the most recently active record but add a confirmation step summarizing the selected order for the customer to approve before any refund is processed.
near_miss_rationale: This still leads with a heuristic guess and puts the burden of catching the error on the customer, who may approve a plausible-looking summary without noticing it references the wrong record. Confirmation is a good safety net, but the correct move on ambiguity is to disambiguate first, not to guess and verify.
distractor1_choice: Escalate to a human agent, since ambiguous customer identity makes the case too risky for autonomous handling.
distractor1_rationale: Multiple matches from a shared email is routine ambiguity the agent can resolve itself with one clarifying question. Escalating every disambiguation wastes human capacity on cases well within the agent's capability and adds latency for the customer.
distractor2_choice: Modify get_customer to return only the single best-scoring match so the agent never has to reason about multiple records.
distractor2_rationale: This hides the ambiguity inside the tool instead of resolving it — the tool now silently makes the same heuristic guess, with the agent (and transcript) unaware an alternative match existed. Ambiguity should surface to be clarified, not be suppressed at the tool layer.
anti_pattern: Selecting one of several matching customer records by heuristic (recency, open orders, best score) instead of requesting an additional identifier to disambiguate before acting.

stem: An analyst runs a single extended agent session to survey 200 vendor security questionnaires. For the first hour the agent's answers cite specific documents ("Vendor 41's questionnaire discloses subprocessors in section 7"). Later in the session, answers drift: the agent describes what vendor questionnaires "typically contain" and gives inconsistent answers to questions it answered correctly earlier, even though the relevant documents were reviewed in-session. What practice most directly counteracts this degradation?
correct_choice: Have the agent maintain a scratchpad file recording key findings as it works, and reference that file when answering subsequent questions instead of relying on earlier conversation context.
correct_rationale: This is context degradation in an extended session: findings discovered early become unreliable to recall, and the model falls back to generic 'typical patterns.' A scratchpad file persists key findings outside the conversation window, giving later questions a durable, re-readable record of what was actually discovered.
near_miss_choice: Periodically summarize the session so far and continue working from the summary in place of the older turns.
near_miss_rationale: Summarization frees context but is lossy in exactly the dangerous way: which of hundreds of specific findings survive each compression pass is not controlled, and dropped specifics are unrecoverable. A findings file is deliberate, structured persistence; a rolling summary is best-effort compression of everything at once.
distractor1_choice: Raise max_tokens on each request so the model can produce more thorough answers that compensate for the fading detail.
distractor1_rationale: Longer answers do not restore lost information — max_tokens governs output budget, and the failure is that discovered specifics are no longer reliably available in the input context. More verbose generic output makes the degradation harder to spot, not better.
distractor2_choice: Switch to a model with a larger context window so the full session history fits without any information loss.
distractor2_rationale: A larger window delays the wall but does not address degradation within long contexts — inconsistent recall and 'typical patterns' answers appear in extended sessions even when history physically fits. Durable structured persistence of findings addresses the failure mode; window size only postpones it.
anti_pattern: Relying on ever-growing conversation context to retain discovered specifics across an extended session, until the model starts citing 'typical patterns' instead of the concrete findings it made earlier.

stem: Your research pipeline runs a coordinator with four subagents over multi-hour jobs. Last night the host was OOM-killed five hours into a six-hour run and all progress was lost; the rerun cost a full extra day. You are asked to make runs recoverable without keeping everything in one process's conversation context. Which design provides crash recovery?
correct_choice: Have each agent export its state — completed work, key findings, pending items — to a known location as a structured manifest, and have the coordinator load the manifests on resume and inject the recovered state into agent prompts.
correct_rationale: Structured state persistence for crash recovery: each agent writes its progress to a known external location, so a restarted coordinator can load a manifest of what was completed and resume from there instead of re-running the whole job. Prompt-injected recovered state rebuilds working context deliberately.
near_miss_choice: Persist every agent's full raw conversation transcript to disk after each turn, and on restart replay the transcripts through the API to reconstruct each agent's context exactly.
near_miss_rationale: Raw transcripts capture everything but are the wrong recovery artifact: replaying hours of verbose tool results re-fills the context with the same bloat, costs a large re-ingestion bill, and much of the transcript is stale exploration rather than durable findings. A structured manifest of completed work and key findings is smaller, current, and directly actionable on resume.
distractor1_choice: Wrap the whole pipeline in automatic process-restart logic so a crashed run is immediately relaunched from the beginning with the same inputs.
distractor1_rationale: Auto-restart without persisted state is exactly what the team is trying to escape — the six-hour job restarts from zero every time. Recovery requires knowing what was already completed, which only persisted state can provide.
distractor2_choice: Move all subagent work into the coordinator's own session so there is only one context to protect, and checkpoint it by asking the model to summarize progress every 30 minutes.
distractor2_rationale: Collapsing subagents into one session destroys the context isolation the architecture depends on and makes the single context the bottleneck. Periodic in-conversation summaries still live inside the process that crashes — they vanish with it unless exported, and as prose they are a lossy substitute for structured state.
anti_pattern: Keeping all pipeline progress inside living conversation contexts with no externally persisted state, so any crash discards hours of completed work.

stem: Your invoice-extraction system routes low-confidence extractions to human review and auto-accepts high-confidence ones. Leadership wants to cut review costs further by trusting high-confidence output entirely and reassigning most reviewers. The pipeline's error rate on auto-accepted extractions was last measured three months ago, before two vendor-format changes rolled out. What should you put in place before reducing human review?
correct_choice: Implement stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions for ongoing human review, measuring the error rate continuously and surfacing novel error patterns as they emerge.
correct_rationale: Auto-accepted output needs ongoing measurement, not a one-time validation: stratified random sampling across document types and fields keeps a continuous estimate of the high-confidence error rate and catches novel error patterns — like those introduced by vendor-format changes — before they silently accumulate.
near_miss_choice: Re-run the validation study from three months ago on the current pipeline, and if accuracy still exceeds the target, approve the reviewer reduction.
near_miss_rationale: A refreshed one-time study only certifies the pipeline against today's document mix; the vendor changes that already happened are exactly the kind of drift that will happen again after the study. The requirement is ongoing error-rate measurement, which a point-in-time re-validation cannot provide.
distractor1_choice: Raise the confidence threshold for auto-acceptance so that only the model's most certain extractions bypass review, making sampling unnecessary.
distractor1_rationale: Raising the threshold routes more documents to a review pool you are trying to shrink, and still leaves the auto-accepted stream unmeasured — high model confidence does not exempt output from novel error patterns, which is why sampled measurement is needed regardless of where the threshold sits.
distractor2_choice: Track the aggregate accuracy metric on the review queue weekly and reduce reviewers as long as it holds above 97%.
distractor2_rationale: The review queue only contains low-confidence extractions, so its accuracy says nothing about the auto-accepted high-confidence stream — the population actually at risk. Aggregate metrics on the wrong population give false assurance while errors flow through unmeasured.
anti_pattern: Reducing human review of auto-accepted extractions on the strength of a stale one-time validation, with no ongoing sampled measurement of the high-confidence stream's error rate.

stem: Your extraction pipeline has the model output a confidence score per extracted field, and routes fields below 0.8 to human review. Reviewers report the routing feels arbitrary: many flagged fields are fine, while a recurring error — transposed digits in account numbers — almost always arrives with confidence above 0.9 and skips review. You have 5,000 documents with ground-truth labels available. How should you fix the review routing?
correct_choice: Calibrate the field-level confidence scores against the labeled validation set — measuring actual accuracy at each score level per field type — and set review thresholds based on the measured relationship rather than the raw scores.
correct_rationale: Self-reported confidence is not inherently calibrated: a 0.9 does not mean 90% accurate, and calibration quality differs by field type. Labeled data lets you measure the true accuracy-versus-score curve per field and place thresholds where they route real risk — including discovering fields like account numbers where the scores are unreliable and review needs different triggers.
near_miss_choice: Raise the review threshold from 0.8 to 0.95 across all fields so that the transposed-digit errors above 0.9 start landing in the review queue.
near_miss_rationale: A blanket threshold change is tuning an uncalibrated signal by anecdote: it floods reviewers with the many accurate 0.8–0.95 fields while still passing any error scored above 0.95, and it ignores that calibration differs per field type. Without measuring scores against labels you cannot know where any threshold should sit.
distractor1_choice: Add an instruction to the extraction prompt telling the model to be more conservative and self-critical when assigning confidence scores.
distractor1_rationale: Prompted humility does not produce calibration — the scores remain self-reported and unanchored to measured accuracy. The systematic blind spot (high confidence on transposed digits) reflects what the model cannot see about its own errors, which no phrasing instruction fixes.
distractor2_choice: Replace confidence-based routing with random sampling: send a fixed 20% of all extracted fields to human review.
distractor2_rationale: Uniform random review spends 80% of reviewer effort on fields that were fine and lets 80% of genuine errors through unreviewed. Random sampling is a measurement tool; for routing limited reviewer capacity, calibrated risk-based routing concentrates review where errors actually live.
anti_pattern: Routing human review on raw self-reported confidence scores without calibrating them against labeled data, leaving systematic high-confidence errors unreviewed.

stem: Your research system's subagents return prose summaries of their sources, and a synthesis agent merges them into a report. Clients now require every claim in the report to be attributable to a source. Spot checks show the synthesis stage is where attribution dies: subagent summaries mention sources inconsistently, and merged paragraphs blend multiple sources so no claim can be traced. Which change fixes attribution end to end?
correct_choice: Require subagents to output structured claim-source mappings — each claim paired with its source URL, document name, and relevant excerpt — and require the synthesis agent to preserve and merge those mappings when combining findings.
correct_rationale: Attribution is lost during summarization when findings are compressed without preserving claim-source mappings. Making the mapping a structured, mandatory part of every subagent's output — and making its preservation an explicit synthesis requirement — carries provenance through the exact stage where prose blending destroys it.
near_miss_choice: Have the synthesis agent add a bibliography section listing every source URL that any subagent consulted during the research run.
near_miss_rationale: A run-level source list is not attribution: it tells the reader which sources existed, not which claim came from which source. The requirement is per-claim traceability, which needs claim-level mappings maintained through synthesis, not an appendix of everything consulted.
distractor1_choice: Instruct the synthesis agent to only include claims it is highly confident about, so unattributable statements are less likely to appear in reports.
distractor1_rationale: Confidence filtering changes which claims survive, not whether surviving claims are traceable — a high-confidence claim with no source mapping is still unattributable. This addresses a different quality dimension and leaves the provenance pipeline broken.
distractor2_choice: Skip synthesis for attribution-sensitive engagements and deliver each subagent's summary as a separate chapter with its own source list.
distractor2_rationale: This abandons the product's value — a unified, cross-source synthesis — to dodge the provenance problem, and chapter-level source lists still don't map individual claims to sources within each summary. Structured claim-source mappings solve attribution without sacrificing synthesis.
anti_pattern: Compressing findings into prose summaries without structured claim-source mappings, so attribution is destroyed at each summarization and synthesis step.

stem: Your market-research pipeline flags "conflicting sources" for human adjudication. Analysts complain that most flagged conflicts are spurious: a typical case flagged "Source A says the market is $2.1B, Source B says $3.4B" — but Source A was published in 2023 and Source B in 2026, so both figures are correct for their time. The subagents' structured outputs currently capture the claim, source name, and URL. What change eliminates this class of false conflict?
correct_choice: Require subagents to include the publication or data-collection date in each structured finding, so downstream stages can interpret differing values as time-series points rather than contradictions.
correct_rationale: Temporal differences get misread as contradictions when findings carry no dates. With publication/collection dates as a required structured field, the conflict detector and synthesis stage can distinguish a genuine disagreement (two sources, same period, different values) from an evolution over time — and can present dated figures as a trend.
near_miss_choice: Prompt the conflict-detection stage to use its judgment about whether two differing figures might both be true before flagging them for adjudication.
near_miss_rationale: Without dates in the findings, the detector has nothing to judge with — it would be guessing at temporality from prose fragments, trading false positives for false negatives. The fix is supplying the missing metadata, after which the temporal check becomes a reliable comparison instead of an inference.
distractor1_choice: When sources disagree, have the pipeline keep the figure from the source with the higher domain-authority ranking and discard the other.
distractor1_rationale: Arbitrarily selecting one value destroys correct information — both figures here are accurate for their periods. Conflicting values from credible sources should be annotated with attribution and, once dated, presented in temporal context; silent discards produce confidently wrong reports.
distractor2_choice: Restrict research subagents to sources published within the last 12 months so all collected figures describe the same period.
distractor2_rationale: A recency cutoff throws away historical data that research often needs (trend analysis, baselines) and still doesn't guarantee comparability within the window — a January figure and a December figure can differ materially. Capturing dates preserves all data and makes temporality explicit.
anti_pattern: Collecting quantitative findings without publication or data-collection dates, so figures from different periods surface as false contradictions.
